Event: Sue pushes people down the stairs when she doesn’t get her way
Explanation: She vents frustration and enforces fear-based control while physically harming others.

Event: Sue punches out a Regionals judge for choosing New Directions
Explanation: The attack vents anger but risks serious consequences and doesn’t change the result.

Event: Sue almost shoots Brittany out of a cannon and rampages when prohibited
Explanation: She endangers Brittany and wrecks property purely out of tantrum, with no clear gain.

Event: Sue gets a boyfriend to deflect suspicion she’s gay
Explanation: She protects her chosen image with minor deception and no evident direct harm to others.

Event: Finn calls Sue’s baby “retarded”
Explanation: He deeply offends Sue over her Berserk Button with no benefit and serious social cost.

Event: Sue slips Figgins a roofie and fabricates blackmail photos
Explanation: She regains power over the school via coerced leverage that humiliates another.

Event: Sue leaks New Directions’ setlists to sabotage them
Explanation: She tries to secure competitive advantage through unethical disclosure that risks punishment.

Event: Sue slashes the tires of Vocal Adrenaline’s bus
Explanation: She commits a crime and risks major consequences just to inconvenience a rival team.

Event: Sue unleashes her real hounds on Vocal Adrenaline
Explanation: She violently intimidates the rival team for revenge, gaining satisfaction and deterrence at their expense.

Event: Sue teams with Will to teach Vocal Adrenaline a lesson
Explanation: She cooperates with an enemy to discipline bullies, protecting others while enjoying payback.

Event: Sue teams with Roz to educate girls about domestic violence
Explanation: She uses her authority to correct harmful attitudes and raise awareness.

Event: Sue resigns as principal in protest of Karofsky’s return
Explanation: She sacrifices her powerful position to stand up for Kurt and against bullying.

Event: Sue expels Karofsky when the death threat is revealed
Explanation: She uses her authority to protect Kurt and other students from a credible threat.

Event: Sue offers Beiste a place to stay after learning of the abuse
Explanation: She gives personal support and shelter to a domestic-violence victim with minimal cost to herself.

Event: Sue goes to great lengths looking after Becky
Explanation: She invests time and care to protect and mentor Becky, strengthening a treasured relationship.

Event: Sue drugs Figgins and stages “tryst” photos to get her job back
Explanation: She regains employment and power via orchestrated humiliation and coercion.

Event: Sue manipulates Figgins into giving New Directions another year
Explanation: She secures their continuation out of respect for Will’s bond with the students.

Event: Sue kicks Becky off the Cheerios after Jean’s death
Explanation: She lashes out at a vulnerable student due to her own grief, hurting both of them emotionally.

Event: Sue reinstates Becky on the Cheerios later
Explanation: She corrects a cruel decision and restores inclusion, rebuilding their bond.

Event: Sue accepts Becky into the Cheerios despite her disability
Explanation: She gives Becky equal opportunity and belonging while gaining a devoted protégé.

Event: Sue treats Becky no differently than other Cheerios
Explanation: She normalizes inclusion and maintains high standards for everyone, benefiting team cohesion.

Event: Sue donates money to build three wheelchair ramps at school
Explanation: She spends her own resources to improve accessibility for disabled students.

Event: Sue reads Little Red Riding Hood to Jean
Explanation: She invests emotionally in comforting her sister, strengthening their relationship.

Event: Sue promises to keep an eye on Karofsky for Kurt’s safety
Explanation: She offers active protection to a bullied student at little cost to herself.

Event: Sue sells a valuable Christmas tree to a toothpick factory
Explanation: She forfeits potential personal gain to turn the asset into cash for others’ benefit.

Event: Sue leaves the tree money at Marley’s house for therapy
Explanation: She quietly funds essential treatment, significantly helping them with no clear downside.

Event: Sue leaves presents for Marley’s family
Explanation: She improves their holiday and preserves therapy funds, at her own expense.

Event: Sue manipulates Will into canceling Rocky Horror with her expose plan
Explanation: Her attempt to win a Local Emmy backfires and only sabotages a production she wanted to condemn.

Event: Sue gets Quinn, Santana, and Brittany to join Glee as moles
Explanation: She seeks intel and sabotage capabilities inside the club for her own advantage.

Event: Sue’s mole plan leads the girls to stay with Glee
Explanation: Her scheme unintentionally strengthens the club and costs her useful minions.

Event: Sue tortures Glee before Regionals, inspiring their winning song
Explanation: Her cruelty motivates them to succeed, improving their outcome and reducing her relative power.

Event: Sue attempts “Sue-icide” as a feint
Explanation: The stunt risks reputational and institutional backlash without giving her clear net benefit.

Event: Sue starts a violent rampage in school when frustrated
Explanation: She vents anger via destruction, risking penalties far outweighing any relief.

Event: Sue tries to win a Local Emmy with a scathing expose on Glee
Explanation: She pursues personal prestige by attacking the club’s show, offering them no benefit.

Event: Sue drugging Figgins in “Sectionals” and denying it to his face
Explanation: She stonewalls accountability to protect herself while undermining authority and trust.

Event: Sue destroys everything in her path when enraged
Explanation: She indulges tantrums through vandalism, inviting serious consequences with no real gain.

Event: Sue salts the earth in her backyard after selling her house
Explanation: She spitefully devalues the property for the next owner while losing any goodwill herself.

Event: Sue tries to marry herself
Explanation: She indulges in a narcissistic act that amuses her and, at worst, bemuses others without real harm.

Event: Sue threatens to pull Artie’s teeth out to extract information
Explanation: She deploys torture threats to gain information, traumatizing him to serve her investigation.

Event: Sue terrorizes the school as principal while boosting academics
Explanation: Her fear regime improves results but harms students’ well-being and autonomy.

Event: Sue calls minority students by demeaning nicknames
Explanation: She asserts dominance and amusement through insults at the cost of their dignity.

Event: Sue repeatedly calls Kurt insulting names like “Lady”
Explanation: She bolsters her persona and power by belittling him, offering him no benefit.

Event: Sue tries to torture Artie at prom to find who spiked the punch
Explanation: She seeks control and answers via intimidation, partially aligned with discipline but abusive.

Event: Sue attempts to pull Artie’s teeth but is stopped
Explanation: The escalating threat risks severe consequences for her while giving little more than she already had.

Event: Sue gives “Reason You Suck” speeches to Will
Explanation: She unloads justified criticism that may also push him to reflect and improve, while satisfying herself.

Event: Sue calls Will out on his treatment of minority and disabled students
Explanation: She highlights real issues that can lead to better treatment, and gains moral high ground.

Event: Sue calls Will out for relying on gimmicks instead of trusting kids
Explanation: Her critique might redirect him toward healthier pedagogy while reinforcing her own savvy image.

Event: Sue confesses the fired gun was hers to protect Becky
Explanation: She sacrifices her job to shield Becky from expulsion or worse, massively helping Becky.

Event: Sue accepts being fired for the gun incident
Explanation: She bears formal punishment so Becky avoids it, taking the institutional hit herself.

Event: Sue gradually becomes a begrudging Team Mom to New Directions
Explanation: She grows into a protective role that gives her connection and benefits the kids’ support system.

Event: Sue does everything to get Blaine and Kurt back together
Explanation: Her extreme meddling achieves the reconciliation she wants and restores their relationship.

Event: Sue builds a Klaine stalker shrine and obsessively ships them
Explanation: Her fixation entertains herself and, in context, indirectly supports their eventual reunion.

Event: Sue uses stalking/Saw-style tactics to reunite Klaine
Explanation: She gets her desired outcome through coercive, frightening methods that traumatize them.

Event: Sue tries to intimidate Will with near-face contact (“Child Star”)
Explanation: She asserts dominance and enjoys unsettling him without offering him any benefit.

Event: Sue destroys the choir room after getting its keys post-Sectionals
Explanation: Her spiteful reaction to their loss only vents anger and damages property, helping nobody.

Event: Sue secures another year for New Directions after they lose at Regionals
Explanation: She uses influence to save her enemies because she values their rivalry and their bonds.
